pages.text: The effect of ﬁbre distribution characteristics on the ﬂexural strength of steel ﬁbre-reinforced ultra high strength concrete Su Tae Kang a, Bang Yeon Lee b, Jin-Keun Kim c, Yun Yong Kim d,⇑ a Structural Engineering & Bridges Research Division, Korea Institute of Construction Technology, 1190 Simindae-ro, Ilsanseo-gu, Goyang 411-712, Republic of Korea b Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, 48105-2125 MI, United States c Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, 335 Gwahangno, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-70 1, Republic of Korea d Department of Civil Engineering, Chungnam National University, 220 Gung-dong, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-764, Republic of Korea article info Article history: Received 15 August 2008 Received in revised form 9 October 2009 Accepted 13 November 2010 Available online 24 December 2010 Keywords: Ultra high strength concrete Steel ﬁbre Fibre distribution Image processing Flexural strength abstract Fibre distribution characteristics were evaluated to investigate their effect on the ﬂexural strength of steel ﬁbre-reinforced ultra high strength concrete in conjunction with the direction of placement. For this purpose, an image processing technique developed in this study was employed. Flexural tests were car- ried out to quantify the effect of ﬁbre distribution characteristics on ﬂexural strength. It was found that the image processing technique developed in this study could quantitatively evaluate the ﬁbre distribu- tion property by the use of the distribution coefﬁcient, the number of ﬁbres in a unit area, the packing density of the ﬁbre image, and ﬁbre orientation. It was also found that the ﬁbre distribution characteris- tics were dependent on the direction of placing. Fibre distribution characteristics were revealed to strongly affect the ultimate ﬂexural strength, while hardly affecting the ﬁrst cracking strength. The valid- ity of the current test results was veriﬁed through comparison with a theoretical model of ﬂexural strength. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction The evolution of concrete technology has attained a level be- yond the classic concept of high strength and has permitted the development of Ultra High Performance Concrete (UHPC). This has solved the problem of brittleness, which can be a critical bar- rier for realizing high strength concrete [1]. The cement-based den- siﬁed matrix commonly known as Reactive Powder Concrete (RPC) is composed of an optimized proportion of particles with diameter below 1 mm and exhibits brittle behavior [2]. Mechanical charac- teristics of steel ﬁbre-reinforced ultra high strength concrete (SFR-UHSC) such as compressive strength, splitting tensile strength, and ﬂexural strength generally range from 180 to 200 MPa, 10 to 12 MPa, and 30 to 35 MPa, respectively. The most important advantages of ﬁbre reinforced concrete are increased tensile strength and improved toughness [3,4]. Furthermore, it im- proves shear strength, increases the resistance against dynamic load, and reduces shrinkage cracking [5,6]. The improvement of such mechanical properties can be achieved through the addition of a moderate amount of properly distributed ﬁbres. This improvement can be maximized by control- ling the alignment and dispersion of continuous long ﬁbres in the matrix. Short ﬁbres with lengths of 10–30 mm are generally adopted in ﬁbre reinforced concrete. These

3. Experiment 3.1. Experimental setup The mix-proportion of SFR-UHSC (198 ± 3.44 MPa in average compressive strength) used in this study is given in Table 2 . The ultimate strength of steel ﬁbre is 2500 MPa, and the diameter and length of the ﬁbre are 0.2 mm and 13 mm, respectively. The property in a fresh state was evaluated by a ﬂow test (ASTM C1437) and the ﬂowability was measured to be about 230 mm. Flexural tests were performed using specimens with dimensions of 100 /C2 100 /C2 400 mm to evaluate the orientation and dispersion of the ﬁbres according to the placing directions of SFR-UHSC. Two cases were considered for the placing directions: placing parallel to the longitudinal direction of the specimen and placing transversely to the longitudinal direction. Fig. 1 illustrates each of the placing directions adopted for the manufacture of the specimens. UHSC specimens without ﬁbres were also tested for comparison. Three test specimens with SFR-UHSC for each case, and four specimens with UHSC without ﬁbres were prepared. Employment of more specimens for UHSC without ﬁbres is due to obtain more reliable value of strength, because the strength is used as a reference value in order to evaluate the effect of the ﬁbre distribution characteris- tics. A four point bending test was adopted for the ﬂexural test. A four point bending test was conducted to examine the rela- tionship between the distribution of the ﬁbres and the tensile char- acteristics of SFR-UHSC. The tests were carried out through displacement control using an actuator with a capacity of 250 kN. The deﬂection at the center of the ﬂexural specimens was measured by means of a LVDT installed at the center of the specimens. Fig. 2 illustrates the ﬂexural test apparatus. To obtain cross-sectional digital images, a bending specimen was cut into four cubes. Each cube was then divided into four equal sized small plates by slicing it transversally, horizontally, and ver- tically, as shown in Fig. 3. High resolution photographs were taken at each of the cutting planes to evaluate the orientation and disper- sion of the ﬁbres. 3.2. Fibre distribution evaluation technique 3.2.1. Detection of ﬁbre The distribution characteristics of ﬁbre can be quantitatively evaluated by calculating the coefﬁcient based on the coordinates of ﬁbres and the shape of the ﬁbres in the cutting plane. To detect the ﬁbre in the ﬁbre images, the RGB image is converted to a gray- scale image. The grayscale image is then converted to a binary im- age based on a set threshold object detection method, which in turn is based on a thresholding algorithm [22]. In this process, other parts aside from the ﬁbres can be detected as ﬁbres due to having similar brightness to ﬁbres. These mis-detected objects are classiﬁed on the basis of the threshold of the object’s area, which is determined by the minimum area of randomly selected ﬁ- bres. Mis-detected objects having smaller area than the threshold area are deleted. The distribution characteristics of the ﬁbre in the detected ﬁbre images are represented quantitatively by the coefﬁcients described in Section 3.2.2. 3.2.2. Distribution coefﬁcients The degree of ﬁbre dispersion is quantitatively evaluated based on calculation of the coefﬁcient af, referred to as the ﬁbre disper- sion coefﬁcient, as expressed by the equation. af ¼ exp /C0 ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ ﬃPðxi /C0 1Þ2 n s2 4 3 5 ð9Þ where n is the total number of ﬁbres on the image and xi denotes the number of ﬁbres in

carried out to quantify the effects of ﬁbre distribution characteris- tics on the ﬂexural behavior, including cracking strength and ulti- mate ﬂexural strength, of SFR-UHSC. The purpose of this work is to present the impact of the ﬁbre distribution, which results from different placing directions of the beam, on the ﬂexural behavior of SFR-UHSC. To this end, ﬂex- ural tests were carried out on specimens manufactured with two different placing directions and ﬂexural behaviors including crack- ing and ultimate ﬂexural strengths were then measured. In order to evaluate the effect of the two different placing directions on the ﬁbre distribution characteristics, an image processing tech- nique was developed and applied. Based on the results, the inﬂu- ence of the ﬁbre distribution characteristics on the mechanical properties, which were represented by ﬂexural strength, of SFR- UHSC was quantitatively evaluated. Based on an image analysis of the ﬁbre distribution, a comparison between the predicted ﬂex- ural strength, assuming uniform distribution of the ﬁbres, and the predicted ﬂexural strength was then performed. 2. Effect of ﬁbre distribution and evaluation method 2.1. Effect of ﬁbre length and orientation The tensile strength of ﬁbre reinforced concrete can be calcu- lated using Eq. (1), assuming perfect bonding between the ﬁbre and matrix on the basis of the ‘‘Rule of Mixture’’. rct ¼ glghVf rfu þ Vmrmt ð1Þ where gl is the coefﬁcient related to the ﬁbre length, and gh is the ﬁbre orientation coefﬁcient. rct, rmt, and rfu are the tensile strength of the composite, the tensile strength of the matrix, and the ulti- mate tensile strength of the ﬁbre, respectively. Vf is the ﬁbre volume fraction and Vm is the matrix volume fraction, equal to 1 /C0 Vf. The coefﬁcient related with the ﬁbre length, gl, is given by For l < lc gl ¼ l 2lc ð2Þ For l P lc gl ¼ 1 /C0 lc 2l ð3Þ where l is the ﬁbre length and lc is the ﬁbre critical transfer length. For frictional stress transfer with a constant interfacial shear stress transfer, the ﬁbre critical transfer length is: lc ¼ rfudf 2sfu ð4Þ where df is the ﬁbre diameter and sfu is the frictional shear bond strength between the matrix and the ﬁbre. The ﬁbre orientation coefﬁcient, gh, should be related to the ﬁ- bre length, the friction acting on the interface between the matrix and the ﬁbre, and ﬁbre local bending in the crack plane. The effect of the ﬁbre orientation on the tensile strength of composite mate- rials has been studied by many researchers [13–15], and the ﬁbre orientation coefﬁcients have been calculated using different assumptions. Table 1 shows calculated results for random 2- and 3-dimensional distributions. It should be noted that the ﬁbre dis- tribution characteristics are generally dependent on the placing direction. Therefore, it is not reasonable to estimate the ﬂexural strength of ﬁbre reinforced concrete by applying gh obtained from the assumption of uniformly distributed ﬁbres. However, there has been little effort to evaluate the impact of the ﬁbre distribution characteristics on the mechanical properties of SFR-UHSC. The present study therefore focuses on the placing direction and resul- tant ﬁbre distribution characteristics, and the relation to the mechanical properties of SFR-UHSC. For the purpose of evaluating the effects of ﬁbre distribution on the mechanical properties of the composite, a ﬁbre orientation coefﬁcient, gh, is adopted based on the suggestion of Xia et al. [16].

aligned parallel to the tensile stress direction; gh = 0 conversely im- plies that all the ﬁbres are aligned perpendicular to the tensile stress direction. g(h) in Eq. (5) can be determined by shape param- eters that represent changes of the ﬁbre orientation from a random distribution to alignment that is parallel or perpendicular to the reference direction. Xia et al. [16] proposed a two-parameter exponential function, given as follows: gðhÞ¼ fsin hg2p/C0 1fcos hg2q/C0 1 R hmax hmin fsin hg2p/C0 1fcos hg2q/C0 1dh ð6Þ where p and q are the shape parameters, which can be used to determine the shape of the probability density function. The param- eters p and q should be more than 1/2. h is in a range from 0 to p/2. 2.2. Fibre distribution evaluation technique Several techniques [17–21], including image analysis, transmis- sion X-ray photography, and AC-IS are available for evaluating the ﬁbre distribution in a composite made of cement matrix and steel, carbon, glass, or organic ﬁbres; i.e., these techniques can be em- ployed to determine the degree of ﬁbre dispersion and orientation in the composite. Among these techniques, image analysis is the most applicable and trusted method to evaluate the distribution characteristics of ﬁbres in a composite [21]. Furthermore, the ori- entation and dispersion of ﬁbres can be evaluated directly without considering any correlation between distribution coefﬁcients and measured values. Therefore, an image processing program to quan- titatively evaluate the distribution characteristics of ﬁbres was developed in the present study. Fig. 2. Flexural tensile test apparatus. The third coefﬁcient is the packing density Fc, which is deﬁned as the division of an object’s area by that of the object’s circum- scribed circle, as expressed by the equation. Fc ¼ Aob Acc ð11Þ The value for Fc tends toward 0 for an extremely elongated ob- ject or 1 for a circular object. Fc is also useful for calculating the in- clined angle of the ﬁbre to the cutting plane ( Fig. 4 ), as expressed by the equation. Fc ¼ pdl=4 pl 2 =4 ¼ d l ¼ d d= cos h ¼ cos h ð12Þ where h, d, and l are the inclined angle of the ﬁbre, the diameter of the ﬁbre, and the major axis length of the ﬁbre image, respectively. 4. Results and discussion 4.1. Flexural test results with directions of placing The ﬂexural test results for SFR-UHSC and non-ﬁbre-reinforced UHSC are shown in Table 3. ‘Non’ indicates plain UHSC without ﬁ- bres, ‘PL’ denotes SFR-UHSC placed parallel to the longitudinal direction of the specimens, which corresponds with the direction of the beam longitudinal axis, and ‘TL’ indicates SFR-UHSC placed transversely to the longitudinal direction of the specimens. The ﬂexural strength of plain UHSC was measured to be 12.68 MPa on average. This is used as the matrix strength for the calculation of composite strength. In terms of the ﬁrst cracking strength, rein- forcing steel ﬁbres resulted in a more than 50% increase in SFR- UHSC composite strength, when compared with plain UHSC strength. Fig. 5 presents the ﬂexural test results with placing direc- tions of SFR-UHSC. Fig. 5 a illustrates the behavior until the initia- tion of cracking and Fig. 5 b presents the overall ﬂexural behavior. SFR-UHSC placed parallel to the longitudinal direction of the spec- imen presents a ﬁrst cracking strength that is 5.5% larger than that of SFR-UHSC placed transversely to the longitudinal direction, and the ﬂexural strength of the former is 61% greater. This means that, even if the initial cracking strength is relatively less inﬂuenced by the orientation and dispersion of the ﬁbres, the effect on the ﬂex- ural strength is nonetheless quite substantial. These results are attributed to the initial cracking strength being largely inﬂuenced by the matrix strength whereas the ﬂexural strength is highly dependent on the ﬁbre bridging strength at the crack face. The pullout resistance of ﬁbres during bond slip has little impact on the ﬁrst cracking, but conversely has a large inﬂuence on the Fig. 6. Fibre images displayed with direction of placement and specimen cutting. ﬂexural strength. The pullout resistance also has a close relation- ship with the ﬁbre length and orientation. This is discussed in the following sections. 4.2. Evaluations of ﬁbre distribution Fig. 6 shows ﬁbre images of the cutting planes with the direc- tion of placement and specimen cutting and Table 4 presents the ﬁbre distribution characteristics using the proposed method out- lined in Section 3.2, displayed with the direction of placement and specimen cutting. In the case of SFR-UHSC placed parallel to the longitudinal direction of the specimen (PL-series), the ﬁbres are more uniformly dispersed in the cross section cut transversely (PL-TC) compared to the others, i.e., PL-HC and PL-VC, as shown in Fig. 6 . Furthermore, most of the ﬁbres in the PL-TC specimens are aligned more parallel to the normal direction of the cutting plane, relative to the other specimens. On the other hand, in the case of placing SFR-UHSC transversely to the longitudinal direction of the specimen (TL-series), the ﬁbre dispersion coefﬁcient ( af = 0.40 in Table 4 ) is approximately 10% higher in the vertical cutting direction (TL-VC) than in the transversal and horizontal cutting directions (specimens TL-TC and TL-HC). Table 4 shows Fn (the number of ﬁbres per unit area) of PL-TC is equivalent to 49, which is approximately three times higher than that of the other PL-specimens, and about 50% higher than that of the TL-TC specimens. In terms of the ﬁbre dispersion coefﬁcient, af of PL-TC (0.38) is 5% higher relative to that of TL-TC, and is the highest value in the PL-series. Conversely, TL-VC exhibits the larg- est Fn, i.e., 36, in the series of TL-specimens, and the largest af (0.40) as well. Fig. 7 shows the Fc histograms and Table 4 shows the aver- age h (the angle between the ﬁbre axis and the normal direction of the cutting plane) calculated from Fc. The average h of PL-TC is 45 /C176, which is approximately 22% lower than that of the other PL-spec- imens, and 18% lower than that of the TL-TC specimens. h is di- rectly related with the effective number of ﬁbres acting at the crack plane, that is, the number of ﬁbres contributing to increased bridging cracking with a decrease of the average value of h of all the ﬁbres at the crack plane. Therefore, the larger values of F n and af, and the lower value of h of PL-TC relative to those of TL- TC imply that the mechanical properties, represented by the ﬂex- ural strength, of the PL beam specimen will be superior to those Fig. 7. (2) From the image analysis results, a noticeable difference in the distribution characteristics was revealed: af, Fn, and h varied with the direction of placing and specimen cutting. Fn and af of PL-TC and TL-VC were larger than those of other cutting planes and the values of PL-TC and TL-VC were lower than those of other cutting planes. Fn and af of PL-TC were larger than those of TL-TC and h of PL-TC was lower than those of TL-TC. This indicates that the ﬂexural strength increases with an increase of the Fn and af values and a decrease of the h value. (3) A comparison between the predicted ﬂexural strength, assuming a uniform distribution of ﬁbres, and the predicted ﬂexural strength, based on an image analysis of the ﬁbre dis- tribution was presented. There was a noticeable difference in the ﬂexural strength between the measured and the pre- dicted values when a uniform ﬁbre distribution was assumed. This implies that the simple assumption of unifor- mity can lead to considerable error in predicting ﬂexural strength, and thus the placing direction of ﬁber reinforced concrete should be taken into account. (4) There was little difference in gh between the values esti- mated directly from an image analysis and the calculated value based on the best ﬁt of p and q, which were introduced to describe the probability density function (PDF) of ﬁbre orientation. This suggests that proper selection of p and q, which can yield a PDF close to the real PDF of the ﬁbre ori- entation, makes is possible to accurately predict the ﬂexural strength without an image analysis on the cross section. of TL beam specimens. This is because the relatively large amount of ﬁbres in the PL specimen contributes to bridging transverse cracks, the normal direction of which is located in the same direc- tion as the beam longitudinal axis. 4.3. Correlation between ﬂexural strength and ﬁbre distribution Eq. (1) in Section 2.1 expresses the uniaxial tensile strength. However, the ﬂexural strength was measured in the present study, and thus the relationship between uniaxial tensile strength and ﬂexural strength needs to be deﬁned in order to compare the mea- sured strength with the estimated strength based on Eq. (1).W e employed a simple relationship between uniaxial and ﬂexural strength, as expressed in Eqs. (15) and (16) [23] . rmft ¼ brmt ð14Þ rcft ¼ arct ð15Þ where rmft and rcft are the ﬂexural strength of the matrix and the composite, respectively. a and b are factors depending on the size of specimen. b is a coefﬁcient that deﬁnes the relationship between the uniaxial tensile strength and ﬂexural strength of the matrix, and generally ranges between 1.3 and 1.5 when the height of the spec- imen is equal to 100 mm. Chanvillard and Rigaud [24] reported that b is as high as 1.42 for a UHPC of similar composition and mechan- ical properties to the material considered in this study. a is a coef- ﬁcient deﬁning the relationship between the uniaxial tensile strength and the ﬂexural strength of composites and is known to be in a range from 1.5 to 2.5 for a similar type of UHPC or SFR-UHSC [24,25]. By substituting Eqs. (15) and (16) into Eq. (1), the following equation can be obtained: rcft a ¼ rf Vf þ 1 b rmft Vm ð16Þ Eq. (17) can then be rewritten as Eq. (18). rcft ¼ arf Vf þ a b rmft Vm ð17Þ In Eq. (18), by substituting rf = glghrfu, the ﬂexural strength of com- posites can be expressed as Eq. (18). rcft ¼ aglghVf rfu þ a b rmft Vm ð18Þ Table 5 shows the ﬂexural strength measured by experiments and calculated by Eq. (18). The values a = 2.0, b = 1.5, gl obtained using Eq. (2), and gh obtained using Eq. (5) are substituted into Eq. (18). a = 2.0 and b = 1.5 are pre-determined by a preliminary experimen- tal study. Fig. 8 shows probability density functions (PDFs) of the angle between the ﬁbre axis and the longitudinal direction of the beam based on an assumption of uniform distribution (PDF unif), the di- rect calculation of gh from the image analysis (PDF meas), and their estimated curves obtained from Eq. (6) with best ﬁts of the shape parameters p and q (PDFﬁt ). Table 5 displays ﬂexural strengths esti- mated based on three types of PDFs along with a comparison with the measured ﬂexural strengths. It can be seen that there is a noticeable difference in ﬂexural strength (Table 5) between the measured and the predicted values based on the assumption of uniform distributed ﬁbres. This implies that the simple assumption of uniformity can lead to considerable error in predicting ﬂexural strength, and thus the placing direction of ﬁber reinforced concrete should be taken into account. As dis- played in Table 5 , the estimation errors of ﬂexural strength were reduced from approximately 18% to 2.5% in the PL-specimens and from 33% to 18% in the TL-specimens. On the other hand, there is little difference in gh (Table 5 ) be- tween the values estimated directly from the image analysis and the values calculated based on the best ﬁt of p and q. This suggests that the proper selection of p and q, which can yield a PDF close to the real PDF of the ﬁbre orien
